worth_it_explanation: Private Marketplace (PMP) and Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deals carry negotiated rates that usually clear above the open auction, so a stalled or mispriced deal trades premium revenue for filler. The check reads the deal records and pricing a publisher already holds in FreeWheel, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and each finding points to a concrete deal a publisher can revive or reprice. IAB Tech Lab reports that roughly two-thirds of deals are set up against the right supply yet generate little revenue, which makes a periodic read of deal health worth the time.
intro: FreeWheel carries PMP and PG deals alongside a publisher's direct-sold and open programmatic demand. A deal is only worth the rate the buyer agreed to if it actually delivers, so a deal that is active in the console but spending nothing, or one whose floor and priority let cheaper demand win the impressions it was meant to fill, returns less than the negotiation promised. This subcategory covers two questions about the deals already running in FreeWheel. The first is whether each deal is healthy, meaning the deal ID is active, mapped to live inventory, and pacing toward its commitment. The second is whether deal floors and priorities are aligned so a deal neither undercuts the publisher's other demand nor gets undercut by it.

audit_checks.title: Deal health is not monitored, so stalled or under-delivering deals go unnoticed
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A PMP or PG deal that is active in the console but not spending returns nothing while occupying inventory the publisher could have filled with other demand. Without a regular read of deal IDs, spend, and pacing, a stalled deal or a PG shortfall can persist for an entire flight before anyone sees it, which trades a negotiated premium rate for open-auction fill or unsold inventory.

educate.base:
A Private Marketplace (PMP) or Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deal in FreeWheel is a negotiated arrangement between a publisher and a named buyer, carried over OpenRTB and identified by a deal ID. A PMP deal invites the buyer into an auction on agreed terms, usually at a negotiated floor. A PG deal goes further. The publisher commits a fixed volume of impressions and the buyer commits to a rate, so a PG deal that under-delivers is a broken commitment on both sides, not just a missed opportunity.

Deal health is the question of whether each deal is doing what the negotiation set up. A healthy deal is active in the console, mapped to live inventory the buyer can actually reach, and pacing toward its expected spend or, for PG, toward its committed volume. A deal can fail any of those quietly. The deal ID can be active but pointed at inventory that no longer carries the audience the buyer wanted. The buyer's seat can be misconfigured so bids never arrive. A PG deal can fall behind pace early in a flight and never recover. None of these surface unless someone reads the deal records against delivery.

The cost of an unmonitored deal is the gap between the negotiated rate and what the inventory earned instead. Premium video and Connected TV (CTV) deals clear well above open-auction prices, so a stalled deal does not just earn zero on that line. It also displaces the chance to fill that inventory with other demand at any price. IAB Tech Lab has reported that roughly two-thirds of deals are configured against the right supply yet return little revenue, much of it traced to configuration errors and unclear deal ownership.

A few signs point to deal health going unmonitored:
  • No regular report lists active deal IDs alongside their spend and pacing over a recent window
  • Deals appear active in the console but show no impressions or revenue against them
  • PG deals are tracked only at the end of a flight, when a shortfall can no longer be corrected
  • Nobody can name which deals are pacing ahead, on track, or behind without opening each one
It may be worth establishing a recurring read of every active deal ID against its spend and pacing, so a stalled PMP deal or a behind-pace PG deal is caught while there is still flight left to correct it.

site_type_overlays.publishing: For a broadcaster or streaming publisher, PG deals often anchor the upfront commitments that sit at the top of the rate card, so a PG shortfall on premium CTV inventory is both lost revenue and a delivery obligation to the buyer. It may be worth watching PG pacing on those marquee deals weekly rather than at flight close since CTV inventory cannot be re-created after the window passes.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a commerce-content publisher running shoppable or product-adjacent video, deals are frequently tied to a sponsor's seasonal window, so a deal that stalls during the window earns nothing and the window does not return. It may be worth confirming that each deal is mapped to the live product or category video inventory the sponsor negotiated since a deal pointed at retired content delivers no impressions even while it reads as active.

learn_more.source_title: IAB Tech Lab Releases Deals API to Standardize Programmatic Deal Sync

educate.learn_more_note: FreeWheel's detailed deal configuration and reporting documentation for the operational console is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: List every active PMP and PG deal ID in FreeWheel, then read each one's spend and pacing over a recent window to find deals that are active but delivering little or nothing.

freewheel_ui.label: FreeWheel console
steps: Open the deals or programmatic section of the FreeWheel console and list every active PMP and PG deal with its deal ID, buyer or seat, and the inventory it targets.
For each deal, confirm the deal ID is active and mapped to inventory that is currently live rather than retired or renamed content.
Record the spend and impressions against each deal over a recent window, and for PG deals note the committed volume alongside delivery to date.
Flag any deal that is active but shows little or no spend, and any PG deal that is behind the pace needed to meet its commitment by flight close.

freewheel_reporting.label: FreeWheel reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by deal ID with impressions, revenue, and average CPM over the recent window.
For PG deals, add committed volume and delivered volume so pacing is visible per deal rather than only in aggregate.
Sort by spend to surface active deals returning little, and compare each deal's average CPM against the rate the deal was negotiated at to confirm it is clearing as agreed.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per active deal ID, whether the deal is mapped to live inventory and what spend and pacing it shows over the recent window, with committed-versus-delivered volume for PG deals. A setup where active deals are mapped correctly and pacing toward their expected spend or committed volume passes. A setup with active deals returning little or no spend, PG deals behind pace with no correction underway, or deals mapped to retired inventory is a fail recorded with the deal IDs and the gap for each.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each active deal ID with its inventory mapping, spend, and pacing above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs Private Marketplace (PMP) and Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deals in FreeWheel without a regular read of deal health, so deals that are active but delivering little go unnoticed. A negotiated deal returns its premium rate only when its deal ID is active, mapped to live inventory, and pacing toward its expected spend or committed volume, and a deal can fail any of those quietly between flight start and flight close. {{voice_recommend}} establishing a recurring report that lists every active deal ID alongside its spend, pacing, and average CPM, with committed-versus-delivered volume for each PG deal, so a stalled PMP deal or a behind-pace PG deal is caught while there is still flight left to correct it. After the report is in place, review it on a cadence short enough to act on a shortfall before the window closes.

audit_checks.title: Deal floors and priorities are not aligned with surrounding demand
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A deal floor set below the publisher's open-auction floor lets a deal win impressions it should have paid more for, and a deal priority set too low lets cheaper open demand outrank a negotiated deal. Either misalignment moves impressions to the wrong line, so the publisher either undercuts its own negotiated rate or fails to honor a deal the buyer expects to win.

educate.base:
A Private Marketplace (PMP) or Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deal in FreeWheel does not run in isolation. It competes against the publisher's open programmatic demand, its other deals, and in many setups its direct-sold line items, and the rules that decide which one wins an impression are the deal's floor and its priority. A deal floor is the minimum price the deal will transact at. A deal priority is where the deal sits in the order FreeWheel resolves demand. When those two settings are aligned with the surrounding demand, the deal wins the impressions it was negotiated to win at the price it was negotiated at. When they are not, impressions move to the wrong line.

The misalignment runs in both directions. A deal floor set below the publisher's open-auction floor lets the deal clear impressions at less than the open market would have paid, so the publisher undercuts itself with its own deal. A deal priority set too low lets cheaper open-auction demand outrank a negotiated deal, so a PMP buyer who agreed to a premium rate loses the impression to a lower bid, or a PG deal falls behind pace because open demand keeps winning ahead of it. The deal can look correctly configured in isolation and still lose every contest it enters because the demand around it is set higher.

FreeWheel resolves deals, open demand, and direct-sold inventory through priority and price together, so the audit question is not whether each deal's floor and priority are reasonable on their own. It is whether they fit the demand they compete against. A PG commitment usually needs a priority high enough to win the volume it guaranteed, and a PMP deal usually needs a floor at or above the open-auction floor so it does not become the cheapest way to buy the inventory.

A few signs point to deal floors and priorities being misaligned:
  • A deal floor sits below the open-auction floor for the same inventory, so the deal is the lowest-priced path to those impressions
  • A PG deal is behind pace while open-auction demand on the same inventory is filling, which points to a priority that lets open demand win first
  • Deal floors and priorities were set once at deal launch and never reconciled against later changes to the open-auction floors or direct-sold priorities
  • Two deals targeting overlapping inventory carry priorities that do not reflect their relative rates, so the cheaper deal can win ahead of the richer one
It may be worth reviewing each deal's floor and priority against the open-auction floor and the direct-sold priority for the same inventory, so a deal neither clears below the open market nor loses the volume it was negotiated to win.

site_type_overlays.publishing: For a broadcaster or streaming publisher, PG upfront commitments on premium Connected TV (CTV) inventory usually need to outrank open programmatic demand because the publisher guaranteed the volume. It may be worth confirming that PG priorities sit above the open-auction path on the inventory they target, so a guaranteed deal is not left behind pace by spot demand winning the same ad pods.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a commerce-content publisher, a sponsor's PMP deal on product or category video is often negotiated at a rate above the open market for that inventory. It may be worth confirming the deal floor sits at or above the open-auction floor for those pages, so the sponsor's deal does not become the cheapest way to reach the higher-intent shopping audience the publisher meant to price up.

educate.learn_more_note: FreeWheel's detailed documentation for setting deal floors and priorities in the operational console is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Compare each deal's floor and priority against the open-auction floor and the direct-sold priority for the same inventory, then check delivery to see whether any deal is winning too cheaply or losing impressions it should win.

freewheel_ui.label: FreeWheel console
steps: For each active PMP and PG deal, open its settings and record its floor, its priority, and the inventory it targets.
For the same inventory, record the open-auction floor and the priority of any direct-sold line items that compete for those impressions.
Compare each deal's floor against the open-auction floor, and flag any deal whose floor sits below the open floor for the same inventory.
Compare each deal's priority against the open-auction path and the direct-sold priority, and flag any PG deal whose priority is not high enough to win its committed volume.

freewheel_reporting.label: FreeWheel reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by deal ID with average CPM and impressions, and compare each deal's average CPM against the open-auction average CPM for the same inventory.
Flag any deal clearing below the open-auction average, which points to a floor set too low relative to surrounding demand.
For PG deals behind pace, check whether open-auction demand on the same inventory is filling at the same time, which points to a priority letting open demand win ahead of the deal.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per deal, its floor and priority alongside the open-auction floor and the competing direct-sold priority for the same inventory, plus delivery evidence such as average CPM versus the open-auction average. A setup where deal floors sit at or above the open floor and deal priorities let each deal win the volume it was negotiated to win passes. A setup with a deal floor below the open-auction floor, a PG deal losing volume to higher-priority open demand, or priorities between overlapping deals that do not reflect their rates is a fail recorded with the deals and the misalignment for each.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each deal's floor and priority alongside the open-auction floor and competing priorities for the same inventory above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the deal floors and priorities at {{site_name}} are not aligned with the demand the deals compete against in FreeWheel, so impressions move to the wrong line. A deal floor below the open-auction floor lets a Private Marketplace (PMP) deal clear below what the open market would have paid, and a deal priority set too low lets cheaper open demand outrank a negotiated deal or leave a Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) commitment behind pace. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing each deal's floor and priority against the open-auction floor and the direct-sold priority for the same inventory, setting PMP floors at or above the open floor and PG priorities high enough to win the committed volume. After the change, recheck average CPM and pacing per deal to confirm each deal clears at its negotiated rate and wins the impressions it was meant to win.
